counties.cobb-county: Cobb County sits southeast of Rome, and Elite Catastrophe serves it in full. When a spring or summer storm line rolls across the county and drops trees on roofs, across driveways, and onto power lines, our family-owned crew answers the call and gets on the road to the damage, available around the clock and year round. Storm work is where the urgency lives, so that is what we lead with: structures first, the tree through the roof, the limb pinning a car, the trunk blocking the only way out, plus storm damage cleanup and roof tarping to keep the weather out until repairs can follow. Cobb carries a heavy tree load, from the mature oaks over older Marietta and Smyrna neighborhoods to the leftover pines in newer south-county subdivisions, so the targets are everywhere. The everyday work is here too: tree removal, tree trimming, and stump grinding, whenever Cobb homeowners need it, storm or no storm.

marietta.narrative: In Marietta, the call often comes right after the sky has finished its worst. A summer storm pushes through Cobb County, and one of the big oaks that has shaded a street near the historic square for years finally lets go, landing across a roof or splitting a driveway. That is the moment Elite Catastrophe moves for. We are a family-owned tree and storm-response crew based up in Rome, and we serve Marietta year round, with the storm emergency as the reason most people first call. When a storm hits Marietta, our work is triage before anything else. Structures come first: the tree through the roof, the limb pinning a car in the drive, the trunk blocking the only way in or out. If the roof is opened up, we can tarp it to hold back the next round of weather until storm roof repair can follow. Marietta's older neighborhoods carry some of the densest mature canopy in Cobb. Water oaks and red oaks planted decades ago now stand taller than the houses they shelter, with heavy limbs reaching over rooflines and power lines, and when straight-line winds and hail work them over the failures come fast. None of that is only a storm-day service, though. The everyday tree work is available around the clock and year round: full removals of trees too far gone to save, trimming broken or overgrown limbs back to safe wood, storm damage cleanup, and grinding out the stumps a takedown leaves behind. Whether the radar is red over Cobb County or the sky is clear, Elite Catastrophe answers calls in Marietta and points the trucks toward town.
marietta.treeAngle: storm-hit mature oaks near the historic square

kennesaw.narrative: Kennesaw grew up in the shadow of its mountain, and the wooded subdivisions filling in around it are heavy with trees. That is beautiful most of the year and a genuine problem for about twenty minutes at a time, when a North Georgia storm line comes through and the wind funnels across the ridges. Elite Catastrophe is a Rome-based, family-owned crew serving Kennesaw, and when a storm hits this part of Cobb County we answer the phone at two in the morning and get on the road. The tree profile in Kennesaw's newer neighborhoods is the classic setup for wind damage. Tall pines that grew up sheltered inside a stand, then were left standing alone after construction cleared the trees around them, now rise exposed in compacted red clay. Add a saturated spring and a hard gust, and those are the pines most likely to end up horizontal, sometimes through a bedroom. Mixed in are maturing hardwoods over the older parts of town that shed heavy limbs under the same conditions. When we roll into Kennesaw after a storm, structures come first: trees on houses, trees on cars, trees blocking a road or a driveway, with roof tarping on hand when a limb has punched through. That storm response is the headline, but it is not the whole service. Removals, tree trimming, storm damage cleanup, and stump grinding are available around the clock and year round, storm or calm, for any homeowner who wants a hazard handled before the weather decides it. The wind-prone trees below Kennesaw Mountain do not wait for a convenient day, and neither do we.
kennesaw.treeAngle: wind-exposed subdivision pines below Kennesaw Mountain

acworth.narrative: Acworth is the shortest drive from our Rome base of any Cobb County city, which matters most on the night a storm has just torn through. This is lake country, wrapped around Lake Acworth and Lake Allatoona, and the wooded lots that slope toward the water are thick with tall trees. When a spring or summer storm crosses the lake and slams into that canopy, the damage can be sudden and severe. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned crew that serves Acworth, and being the closest Cobb city to us simply means we can usually reach a storm here faster. Lakeside lots bring their own hazards. Trees on a slope anchor in thinner, wetter soil, they lean toward the open water and the light, and when the wind pours across the lake they catch the full force of it. Loblolly pines uproot out of saturated red clay, mature oaks lose big limbs, and what falls tends to fall toward the house at the top of the lot. Our storm response in Acworth is structures-first every time: the tree on the roof, the limb through a window, the trunk across the only driveway down to the water. When a roof takes a hit we can tarp it against the next band of weather. That urgent work leads, but it does not stand alone. Tree removal, tree trimming, storm damage cleanup, and stump grinding are all available around the clock and year round, so an Acworth homeowner can take down a leaning lakeside pine on a calm afternoon instead of waiting for the storm to make the call. When Cobb County weather turns dangerous, Elite Catastrophe runs those calls anytime.
acworth.treeAngle: wooded lakeside lots on Lake Acworth slopes

smyrna.narrative: Smyrna packs a lot of house and a lot of tree into a small footprint. The Jonquil City is one of the denser inner suburbs of Cobb County, with mature street trees lined up close over sidewalks, driveways, and rooftops that sit only a few feet apart. That closeness is exactly what makes storm damage here so unforgiving: when a tree comes down in Smyrna, there is rarely open ground for it to land on, so it lands on something built. Elite Catastrophe serves Smyrna from our Rome base, and we make the drive because a family in a tight neighborhood with a tree through the roof needs a crew that does this work day in and day out. The mature street trees that give Smyrna its character are also the ones that fail into houses. Older water oaks and red oaks carry heavy limbs over rooflines, and when hail and straight-line winds work them over, or the red clay softens under a week of rain, big wood comes down in the worst possible place. Taking trees off structures in a dense neighborhood is precision work: limbs come down in pieces, in the right order, with no room for error between two close houses. When a storm hits Smyrna we handle the emergency first, tarp any opened roof to keep the rain out, then clear the storm damage. And the calmer work is here on the same schedule, available around the clock and year round: tree removal, tree trimming, and stump grinding for the leaning oak or the crowded pine you would rather deal with today than during the next warning. When the radar turns red over Cobb, Elite Catastrophe answers, and it answers on the quiet days too.
smyrna.treeAngle: close-set mature street trees over tight lots

powder-springs.narrative: Powder Springs is growing fast, and much of that growth happened right in among the trees. New neighborhoods here are carved out of wooded ground and set into the heavy red clay that defines this corner of Cobb County, and that combination is what puts the town on watch every spring. Saturated clay loses its grip on a root plate, a straight-line wind leans on an exposed tree, and something that stood straight yesterday is on the house today. Elite Catastrophe is a Rome-based, family-owned company serving Powder Springs, and the storm is what most people are calling about when they first reach us. The trees that cause the most trouble in these newer subdivisions are the leftover pines. They grew up crowded and slender inside a stand, then construction took away the trees around them and left them tall, thin, and exposed, rooted in clay that was graded and packed hard. A wet week plus a hard gust, and those are the pines that snap or uproot onto a roof. When a storm hits Powder Springs, structures come first, always: the tree on the house, the limb over the front door, the trunk blocking the drive, with roof tarping and storm damage cleanup right behind. That is the urgent lane, and it leads for a reason. But the full service runs year round and around the clock: tree removal of what is too far gone, tree trimming to bring broken or overgrown limbs back to safe wood, and stump grinding so the yard is usable again. Storm or clear sky, the crew that answers is the same, and Powder Springs can call it anytime.
powder-springs.treeAngle: storm-toppled leftover pines in red-clay subdivisions

austell.narrative: When a storm parks over Austell, the town gets hit two ways at once. Wind and hail work the trees while Sweetwater Creek and the low bottoms take on water, and a tree standing in soft, saturated ground near the creek does not need much of a gust to come over. Austell mixes an older town core with newer growth on the edges, so the hazards run the full range. Elite Catastrophe serves Austell from Rome, a family-owned crew that answers the call and drives to the damage when severe weather turns those trees into emergencies. The flood-prone bottoms are what set Austell apart. Ground that stays wet for days holds trees loosely, and the loblolly pines and creek-side hardwoods rooted in it are prime candidates to uproot when the wind arrives on top of the water. Up in the older neighborhoods, mature oaks over close-set houses fail the way old oaks do, shedding heavy limbs onto rooflines and cars. Whatever brought the tree down, the order of work stays the same when a storm hits Austell: structures first. The trunk through the roof, the limb pinning a vehicle, the tree blocking the only road out of a low-lying street, all of it ahead of anything cosmetic, with roof tarping to hold a damaged roof until repairs can be made. Once the emergency is stable, the everyday work is right there and available around the clock and year round: tree removal, tree trimming back to safe wood, storm damage cleanup, and stump grinding. You do not have to wait for the creek to rise or the sky to open. Elite Catastrophe works Austell on the calm days and the hard ones alike.
austell.treeAngle: creek-bottom trees uprooting in saturated ground

mableton.narrative: Mableton is one of Georgia's newer cities, incorporated only recently in south Cobb County, though the trees standing over it have been here far longer than the city limits. That mix is the whole story: established older neighborhoods with mature canopy sitting right alongside newer subdivisions full of leftover pines. When a North Georgia storm crosses south Cobb, both kinds of tree fail, and Elite Catastrophe is the crew serving Mableton when the wreckage needs clearing. We come out of Rome, a family-owned storm-response and tree company, and we answer the phone around the clock and year round, driving to wherever the damage is. In the older parts of Mableton, mature oaks and sweetgums over settled neighborhoods drop heavy limbs and split apart under hail and straight-line wind. In the newer subdivisions, the tall pines left standing after construction lean and snap out of saturated red clay. Either way, when a storm hits Mableton the priority holds: structures first. Trees on houses, trees on cars, trees across the road or the driveway, handled ahead of anything that can wait, with roof tarping and storm damage cleanup to close up what the storm opened. That emergency response is what we lead with, because it cannot wait. The rest of the work is no less available: tree removal, tree trimming back to safe wood, and stump grinding, on a clear Tuesday just as readily as after a warning. A homeowner in Mableton does not have to time a hazard tree to a storm. Elite Catastrophe serves the city in full, and the road from Rome stays open every day of the year.
mableton.treeAngle: mixed old canopy and leftover south-Cobb pines
